perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of Minnesota Power, the Custom er shall return to Minnesota Power all docum entation and electr onic m edia containing Inform ation and shall provide written certif ication that copies of all Inf ormation have been destroyed or returned.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable request of Minnesota Power, the Customer will attempt to return documentation and electronic media containing Information, or provide a good faith effort to certify the destruction or return of all Information copies.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "written request" with "reasonable request" introduces ambiguity on what is deemed reasonable. Replacing "shall return" and "shall provide" with "will attempt to return" and "good faith effort" weakens the obligation to return or certify destruction, making it non-mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02 defines misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to return or certify the destruction of confidential information, the revised language makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation. The "good faith effort" standard introduces subjectivity, potentially conflicting with the statute's intent to prevent unauthorized use of trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to return or certify the destruction of confidential information, creating a contradiction. The original contract states the customer 'shall return' and 'shall provide,' while the changed text uses 'will attempt to return' and 'good faith effort.' This weakens the obligation, making it non-mandatory. Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02 allows for injunctive relief in cases of actual or threatened misappropriation. By weakening the obligation to return or certify destruction, proving misappropriation becomes more difficult. The 'good faith effort' introduces subjectivity, potentially conflicting with the statute's intent to prevent unauthorized use of trade secrets. The statute also allows for 'affirmative acts to protect a trade secret' to be compelled by court order; weakening the return obligation undermines this provision.

perturbation.original_text: Minnesota Power shall furnish to Custom er, by any m eans or m edium , the proprietary, non-public inform ation concerning Minnesota Power’s Paym ent Schedule.
perturbation.changed_text: Minnesota Power may furnish to Customer, through means it considers reasonable, some information possibly related to Minnesota Power's Payment Schedule.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall furnish" to "may furnish" makes the provision discretionary. Replacing "proprietary, non-public information" with "some information possibly related to" drastically reduces the scope and clarity of the information being provided. This introduces substantial ambiguity about Minnesota Power's actual obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325D.44 Subd. 1(5) pertains to deceptive trade practices. If Minnesota Power intentionally provides vague or misleading information under the revised clause, it could be argued that they are engaging in a deceptive trade practice by not fully disclosing the necessary details, thereby creating a false representation about the benefits of the Distributed Generation Service.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325D.44 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325D Section 325D.44 325D.43 325D.45 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325D Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325D.44 Version List Recent History 2025 Subd. 1a Amended 2025 c 20 s 247 2024 Subd. 1a New 2024 c 111 s 1 2024 Subd. 1b New 2024 c 111 s 2 2023 Subd. 1 Amended 2023 c 57 art 4 s 6 2023 Subd. 2 Amended 2023 c 57 art 4 s 7 This section has been affected by law enacted during the 2025 Regular Session. More info... 325D.44 subd. 1a has been amended by Chapter 20, Section 247 Note: see session law sections for effective dates. 325D.44 DECEPTIVE TRADE PRACTICES. § Subdivision 1. Acts constituting. A person engages in a deceptive trade practice when, in the course of business, vocation, or occupation, the person: (1) passes off goods or services as those of another; (2) causes likelihood of confusion or of misunderstanding as to the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services; (3) causes likelihood of confusion or of misunderstanding as to affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another; (4) uses deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services; (5) represents that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person does not have; (6) represents that goods are original or new if they are deteriorated, altered, reconditioned, reclaimed, used
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall furnish" to "may furnish" significantly weakens the obligation to provide information, introducing ambiguity. Additionally, replacing "proprietary, non-public information" with "some information possibly related to" introduces further ambiguity. This contradiction exists because the original contract mandated the furnishing of specific information, whereas the changed contract makes the provision of any information discretionary and vague. Per Minnesota Statutes § 325D.44 Subd. 1(5), representing that services have benefits that they do not have constitutes a deceptive trade practice. The Law Explanation suggests the provision of vague or misleading information could be argued as a deceptive trade practice. While the scraped snippet doesn't directly address information requirements, it provides a foundation for understanding that intentionally vague information that misrepresents the benefits of a service can lead to legal issues, suggesting a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective for a term of ______ (__) years from the day first written above. Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure (including the provisions relating to the breach of this Agreement) or use of the Information shall remain in full force and eff ect for ________ years or as long as the Information is confidential and non-public, whichever is less, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be effective for a term of what seems like several years from the date written above. Despite possible future agreements, the provisions regarding nondisclosure should probably remain effective for a few years, or perhaps until the Information is no longer really that confidential, unless the parties perhaps agree otherwise in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall be effective" with "may be effective" creates ambiguity about the agreement's enforceability and validity period. Using phrases like "what seems like several years," "should probably remain effective," "a few years," and "no longer really that confidential" makes the duration and scope of the agreement's key provisions extremely vague and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-201: Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-201 requires contracts for the sale of goods for $500 or more to be in writing and clearly specify the terms, including duration. While this agreement isn't *explicitly* for the sale of goods, the distributed generation service arguably involves a form of energy exchange. More importantly, the vagueness surrounding the agreement's duration directly undermines the statute's purpose of providing certainty in contractual obligations. The extremely vague language makes it difficult to determine the agreement's duration.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.2-201 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.2-201 336.2-107 336.2-202 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.2-201 Version List Recent History 2024 336.2-201 Amended 2024 c 93 art 2 s 3 336.2-201 FORMAL REQUIREMENTS; STATUTE OF FRAUDS. (1) Except as otherwise provided in this section a contract for the sale of goods for the price of $500 or more is not enforceable by way of action or defense unless there is a record sufficient to indicate that a contract for sale has been made between the parties and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought or by the party's authorized agent or broker. A record is not insufficient because it omits or incorrectly states a term agreed upon but the contract is not enforceable under this subsection beyond the quantity of goods shown in the record. (2) Between merchants if within a reasonable time a record in confirmation of the contract and sufficient against the sender is received and the party receiving it has reason to know its contents, it satisfies the requirements of subsection (1) against the party unless notice in a record of objection to its contents is given within ten days after it is received. (3) A contract which does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (1) but which is valid in other respects is enforceable (a) if the goods are to be specially manufactured for the buyer and are not suitable for sale to others in the ordinary course of the seller's business and the seller, before notice of repudiation is received and under circumstances
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces significant ambiguities regarding the agreement's duration and the enforceability of its key provisions. This directly undermines the purpose of Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-201, which requires contracts for the sale of goods of $500 or more to be in writing and clearly specify the terms, including duration. Although the Law Explanation states this agreement may not be *explicitly* for the sale of goods, it could involve a form of energy exchange, but more importantly, the scraped snippet from MN Statute 336.2-201 states that a contract is not enforceable unless there is sufficient record indicating that a contract for sale has been made between the parties. The ambiguous language used in the changed text regarding duration and obligations, replacing definitive terms with vague phrases like 'what seems like several years' and 'should probably remain effective,' makes it difficult to determine the agreement's actual terms and thus creates a significant contradiction by failing to meet the basic requirements for enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement, and all transactions under it, shall be governed by the laws of the State of Minnesota, without regard to Minnesota law governi ng conflict of laws, even if one or more of the Parties hereto may be a resident of, or domiciled in, any other state or country.
perturbation.changed_text: The general understanding of this Agreement's construction, interpretation, and performance, along with related activities, might generally align with Minnesota law, but possibly without strict adherence to its conflict of laws principles, even if the Parties are located elsewhere.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall be governed by" with "might generally align with" significantly weakens the governing law provision. Introducing subjective terms like "general understanding" and "possibly without strict adherence" creates ambiguity about which laws actually apply and to what extent, making the agreement's legal framework uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 541.05 establishes statutes of limitations for various legal claims. By creating ambiguity about which state's laws apply, the revised clause also introduces uncertainty about which statute of limitations governs disputes arising from the agreement. This could potentially violate the principle of providing a clear timeframe within which legal actions must be initiated, creating potential legal issues.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 541.05 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes CIVIL PROCEDURE Chapter 541 Section 541.05 541.04 541.051 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 541 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 541.05 Version List Recent History 2015 Subd. 1 Amended 2015 c 5 art 15 s 18 2000 Subd. 1 Amended 2000 c 471 s 1 541.05 VARIOUS CASES, SIX YEARS. § Subdivision 1. Six-year limitation. Except where the Uniform Commercial Code otherwise prescribes, the following actions shall be commenced within six years: (1) upon a contract or other obligation, express or implied, as to which no other limitation is expressly prescribed; (2) upon a liability created by statute, other than those arising upon a penalty or forfeiture or where a shorter period is provided by section 541.07 ; (3) for a trespass upon real estate; (4) for taking, detaining, or injuring personal property, including actions for the specific recovery thereof; (5) for criminal conversation, or for any other injury to the person or rights of another, not arising on contract, and not hereinafter enumerated; (6) for relief on the ground of fraud, in which case the cause of action shall not be deemed to have accrued until the discovery by the aggrieved party of the facts constituting the fraud; (7) against sureties upon the official bond of any public officer, whether of the state or of any county, town, school district, or a municipality therein; in which case the limitation shall not begin to run until the term of such officer for which the bond was given shall have expired; (8) for damages caused by a dam, used for commercial purposes; or (9) for assault, battery, false impriso
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly states that Minnesota law "shall be governed by" the agreement. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the agreement "might generally align with Minnesota law", weakening the governing law provision. The Law Explanation states that Minnesota Statutes § 541.05 establishes statutes of limitations, and the ambiguity created by the change introduces uncertainty about which statute of limitations governs the disputes arising from the agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows that section 541.05 covers "VARIOUS CASES, SIX YEARS." and includes actions "upon a contract or other obligation". The modified text creates ambiguity regarding which jurisdiction's laws apply, and therefore which statute of limitations applies, potentially violating the principle of providing a clear timeframe for legal actions. This aligns with the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition, where an ambiguous legal obligation is created making it difficult to enforce under state law.
